posts.name: Joel Singer
posts.cooked: After collecting red burgundies for a number of years, I got the white burg bug a few years ago. While, I’ve been buying recent vintages, I’ve been reluctant to buy older wines at auction due to concerns about premox. As a result, I’ve purchased almost no wines from before 2006 and mostly have focused on wines from 2010 onward. Has premox influenced other people’s purchasing strategy?

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked: Massive change. I probably own less than 1/10 of the white Burg that I would own if not for the PremOx issues…

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Peter Hirsch:

Massive change. I probably own less than 1/10 of the white Burg that I would own if not for the PremOx issues…

Don Cornwall has a major premix site, yes? But does it have a Premox 101 or where is the best place to go for that?

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
RichardFlack:
Peter Hirsch:

Massive change. I probably own less than 1/10 of the white Burg that I would own if not for the PremOx issues…

Don Cornwall has a major premix site, yes? But does it have a Premox 101 or where is the best place to go for that?

Good site

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I stopped for awhile, but I’m back with a new plan that is working for me.

I’m basically cherry picking good buying opportunities (many from board member Don Eschev) in the $40-60 range or so, a mix of village and 1er Cru, and just drinking them young. I’m mostly drinking 2011-14 vintages recently.

I am just not finding that these wines need age, certainly not such that it’s worth playing premox roullette. And I think they compete well with good new world chardonnay around that price range.
I’m buying less and consuming them younger. I buy almost no grand cru. I’ve also shifted to more white Bordeaux, Austrian, and Germans.

If there was NO premox issues i’d Be buying a lot more White Burgundy. As it is…I think I’m buying about 12 bottles a year with the intent of consuming them in 1-2 years. I buy no Grand Crus and very few 1ers. If it was not a problem…i’d Probably be buying 4-6 cases/year from top to bottom. Now Champagne, Germany, Rhône, & the Loire gets allmost all my attention for whites.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. I also buy Ramonet, hoping they’ve fixed the problems and believing that the highs are high enough to justify the risk at the very reasonable UK allocation pricing. Dauvissat too, which I don’t mind drinking on the younger side. And I’m buying a lot of Guffens-Heynon Mâconnais wines, which have the cut and amplitude of the Côte de Beaune’s best but also show a lot of what they have to give after just five or six years (though my experience with vintages from the 1990s has been positive too). The best producers in the Mâconnais and Côte Chalonnaise have a lot to offer in the premox era, as the problem has neutralized many of the Côte de Beaune’s hypothetical advantages.

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: Bourgogne blanc drunk within 3-4 years, village/premier cru chablis also drunk early and the rare 1er & GC drunk no later than 5 years by producers who have a good track record.

posts.name: Chris. Mc Cart
posts.cooked: Yes, massive change about 10 years ago. Very minor purchases since then and zero GC Beaunes or Chablis.

posts.name: Paul Chiu
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. William: May I ask why you are very disappointed in Raphael’s from 2013?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Paul Chiu:
William Kelley:

This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. I also buy Ramonet, hoping they’ve fixed the problems and believing that the highs are high enough to justify the risk at the very reasonable UK allocation pricing. Dauvissat too, which I don’t mind drinking on the younger side. And I’m buying a lot of Guffens-Heynon Mâconnais wines, which have the cut and amplitude of the Côte de Beaune’s best but also show a lot of what they have to give after just five or six years (though my experience with vintages from the 1990s has been positive too). The best producers in the Mâconnais and Côte Chalonnaise have a lot to offer in the premox era, as the problem has neutralized many of the Côte de Beaune’s hypothetical advantages.

William: May I ask why you are very disappointed in Raphael’s from 2013?

Simply, they don’t taste like Coche any more. In the glass, the difference is glaringly obvious. I would expand, however, I realize it’s always difficult for a son to fill the shoes of a father like Jean-François, so I do not want to be polemical about it. But with '13, '14, and '15 looking like swings-and-misses chez Coche, I am pretty concerned about what the future holds for this great Domaine.

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked: I’m a novice to white Burgundy but have just resorted to buying it off wine lists and letting the restaurant take the risk. For example I recently found 2006 Bonneau du Martray Corton-Charlemagne on a wine list; per Don’s wiki it is something “to now be avoided like the plague.” But the somm promised it would be good, and so it was Certainly a much less stressful experience than if it was my own bottle

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Like many here, not buying anything very old. Still buying 2007+ and bits of new vintages but my intent is not to age more than 5-10 years. I’m really enjoying 7/8/11 now, but Bourgogne Blance, village and 1ers only. I’m also tasting more similar profile wines from other regions like Macon, plus others like Loire, Germany, and coastal California (i.e far west Sonoma, Santa Cruz mountains).

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I buy less, always have a backup, and bitch a lot more. The highs are still so high.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
Joel Singer:

After collecting red burgundies for a number of years, I got the white burg bug a few years ago. While, I’ve been buying recent vintages, I’ve been reluctant to buy older wines at auction due to concerns about premox. As a result, I’ve purchased almost no wines from before 2006 and mostly have focused on wines from 2010 onward. Has premox influenced other people’s purchasing strategy?

The caution I would offer is that since 2006 there have been instances where previously low incidence premox producers have suddenly become hotbeds for the phenomenon. Domaine Leflaive is a key example. On both quality and perceived near-immunity to premox, Leflaive’s wines have become about the most expensive in the marketplace, and yet starting with the 2007 vintage the premox problem has suddenly become quite serious per anecdotal evidence.

Best to keep a watch on Don’s wiki- I follow and post there and it is a tremendous resource- as that site is the best centralized location offering frequent evaluations and unbiased conclusions.

For my part- I stepped away for a while, but there is just nothing on this earth like a great mature white burgundy. So what I have done is select a very small number of wines that I love most where I also have observed lower levels of premox, and then I buy at least 6 bottles (sometimes 5 if that is the best I can do) of each wine instead of taking 3-4 bottles each from a wider array of a producer’s portfolio.

For me, these days that means 3 wines from Ramonet and 2 wines from Bouzereau. I would like to add Sauzet back into the mix again too, but I need to see a bit stronger history develop since a time when they were among the most problematic.

Overall, this means my actual purchases in terms of numbers of bottles each vintage is down about 50% from where it was until the mid 2000s.

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked: I used to begin mid-morning thinking about what white Burgundy I might have with dinner; those days and that mindset are long gone. And as much as I love the wines, I never thought that they had no qualitative equals, and I can’t think of any of the other excellent white wines out there that are priced so ambitiously.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: As a general statement, they don’t need age nearly as much these days anyway. Most white Burgs are ripe, open, and not scorchingly acidic nowadays - there just isn’t a strong incentive to age them into No Man’s Land anymore.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked: I buy regularly the new Chablis releases of Dauvissat, some Raveneau backfilling, Fevre as earlier drinkers, Louis Michel 14’s and others. However, I consume such that 2010 is my earliest vintage. If anything, premox has caused me to allow my California chards to age while I consume white burgs- mostly Ceritas and some Rhys sits. I buy a fair amount of Corton Charlemagne from various producers. For all these I watch CT notes for any signs of advancement.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked: I am not convinced that any producer is spared if given the enough time in the cellar, except maybe Raveneau, and I can’t find those wines at decent retail prices as opposed to grey market prices. Used to drink Coche, but again won’t pay grey market and sorry to hear that the wines under the new regime may not be the same. Leflaive used to be my go-to spared producer but are now patheticly poxed…unless you drink them upon release. I love mature white Burgundy, and if you can’t age them reliably then they ain’t worth the tariff IMO. So I drink Bourgognes by the case and long for the old days.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Peter Hirsch:

Massive change. I probably own less than 1/10 of the white Burg that I would own if not for the PremOx issues…

Yeah, this.

Buying way less, drinking less, and drinking them much earlier.

Amazing that prices keep going up, but the issues STILL persist…


We had 4 Raveneau’s last night, from 1997-2000 (the 2000 was oxy), but the other wines were so super fresh, everyone was at least 10 years out in their assessment.

Guess we are forgetting what WB USED to look like.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
I have been more focused on selling . I sold all my Leflaives for example . Lot’s of Dauvissat’s . I am amazed how much you get for these wines . Now , I just follow a few domaines where my premox rate has been acceptable : Coche Dury , Raveneau , Henri Boillot ( border line ) . Or I buy whites for earlier drinking such as Fabien Coche . So I buy much less .

Regarding Coche Dury , I agree there is a change . The typical spicy Coche nose is gone and this is a real pitty . The wines are cleaner and more pure but less interesting.
But it is too early to say that there is a total style change or whether this was because of the vintage . ( Rafael also doubled prices in just a few years but I can understand that , why should the middle men make all the profit ).

There are new domaines in white Burgundy that make wines that imho are totally stunning , but because of the small quantities very difficult to get and remain of the radar .

For example : last week , I visited Domaine Lamy-Caillot in Chassagne . Their wines were the best Chassagne premier cru’s I ever tasted . The domaine is extramely small : 1,2 ha .
The owners are a couple of very dedicated people . They do have Caillerets and La Romanee . Their hope is to increase their holdings over time ( wife is part of the Lamy-Pillot family ).
This is the real fun of Burgundy , finding the new hidden gems .

Another one ? Domaine de Cerberons in Meursault . Lady wine maker , she has a Les Cras . Style is somewhere between d’Auvenay and Henri Boillot . No sophistication but amazing concentration .

In Burgundy , there is always a new generation coming up with stunning wines . Unfortunately , they are very quickly discovered and then the wines are no longer available or too expensive . So to get an allocation on time is critical . Because the prices ex-domaine are usually reasonable .

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: Only BBs, villages and 1ers from certain producers and all drunk within 5-10 years. Only producer I buy widely is pycm but I still drink within 10 years. Switched a big chunk to dry German Riesling.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: It’s been over 5 years since I bought a white Burgundy. It was a painful breakup but she was just too unfaithful.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
German riesling much cheaper…as well as Alsace indeed
Ref Burgundy, important is wines drinking well when young. For instance, drank a Fontaine Gagnard 2014 Batard and it was fabulous while we had a Dancer Chevalier Montrachet 2013 at Trompette last week which was very impressive but in need of many years, so forget it, I suppose…
Ref BB, this is if you want Burg as many, while very good, are hardly exciting… and you may find some more exciting wines from other regions (Loire)
I concur with Dan on having Villages and 1er crus and on PYCM. I also buy a very few bottles of Lafon (1 1er and 2 Vill), 1 Ente and 2 Coche Dury… a year at intermediate prices (less than secondary)

But I went mad on 2014s whites as they drink so well including Chablis (Dauvissat, Billaud simon), Meursault (Darviot Perrin, Buisson Charles, Fichet), Chassagne (B Moreau, DP) et St Aubin (Lamy, PYCM but also Corton Charlie…)

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Others may get satisfaction from young white Burgs, but for me white Burgs that drink well when young misses the whole point of white Burgundy, which like red Burgundy or Bordeaux, is the development of a magical tertiary complexity. Without that, why bother?

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Robert Grenley:

I love mature white Burgundy, and if you can’t age them reliably then they ain’t worth the tariff IMO.

My feelings exactly. Mature white Burgundy is magical. Young white Burgundy can be very good but IMO is overpriced for what you get. With the exception of an occasional pre-1995 bottle at auction and an occasional Bourgogne for drinking young I’m out of the market.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Herwig Janssen:

For example : last week , I visited Domaine Lamy-Caillot in Chassagne . Their wines were the best Chassagne premier cru’s I ever tasted . The domaine is extramely small : 1,2 ha .
The owners are a couple of very dedicated people . They do have Caillerets and La Romanee . Their hope is to increase their holdings over time ( wife is part of the Lamy-Pillot family ).
This is the real fun of Burgundy , finding the new hidden gems .

You mean Lamy-Caill**a**t, Herwig! I totally agree with you about the quality, it’s very exciting. I’m quite proud to have been (I believe) the first Anglophone reviewer to write about them. Fingers crossed they are able to get more appellations - Lamy-Pillot have some superb holdings.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: Yes, Im actually buying more, but from only a few stellar producers such as Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey and his wifes label under Caroline Morey, plus Morey-Coffinet and Hubert Lamy. They are so good out of the gate and yes, I love mature white Burgundy and fortunately have many from past purchases to just hold on and enjoy them from time to time.

posts.name: Joel Singer
posts.cooked: Thanks for all the thoughtful replies. So far my strategy (read “luck”) has worked ok. Aside from 2 badly oxsidised 2005 Jadot Corton Charlies and a premoxed 2007 Matrot Meursault Perrieres, I’ve been able to enjoy my purchases (touch wood). I do rely heavily on the oxisided-burgs wiki in making my buying decisions. I am particularly interested in people’s experience with Henri Boillot, a producer whose wines I like and can mostly afford. The wiki lists him as a Category II producer (Above-average Premox Incidence), but the notes on bottles for the 2008 vintage and beyond, where I focus, show many solid bottles with only a few oxidised.

posts.name: jon leifer
posts.cooked: I have limited my white burg buying for the past 5-6 yrs to Macon,St Veran, Pouilly Fuisse and the occasional Les Setilles from Olivier LeFlaive…Most of my whites come from Greece, Italy, California plus some odds and ends from Germany, Long Island and the Finger Lakes

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
jon leifer:

I have limited my white burg buying for the past 5-6 yrs to Macon,St Veran, Pouilly Fuisse

A similar story here. Just as I was about to expand my interest in white burgs, the double whammy of premox and escalating prices hit. It absolutely stopped that interest in it’s tracks. I still remain amazed at the two going hand in hand, and remain convinced it left too many in the region ‘fat, dumb and happy’, rather than treating the problem as if it endangered their livelihood.

The advantage of the wines you name, is that whilst some can age for a good decade, they are often rewarding well before that. I’ve yet to experience any hint of premox. The options of drinking wines at twice or more of the price, which may be too primary, or holding longer in fear makes their more lauded neighbours a poor alternative for me.

I buy same amount of white Burg, but now I refuse to spend too much on it, so no more Leflaive, no more Chevalier/Batard/Montrachet.

I still age them as usual, around 5 years for village, 8-10 years for 1er cru, 10-15 for GC. I know that I will have some casualties, but it is so beautiful when it is at its peak that I have hard time drinking them early

I have stopped Leflaive. My go to domaines are Niellon, Dauvissat, Boillot, Buisson Charles, Bonneau du Martray and Bouchard P&F. I have yet to have an oxydized Boillot.
The worst for me was Leflaive, by far.

Yea, my experience with premox the past few years has pretty much stopped my purchases. I would say that 75% of my flawed bottles are premox white burgundy.

Someone else said it above but if it is not age-worthy why pay the premium?

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: it has curtailed a bunch of white burgundy purchasing for me. I have about 18-24 bottles of white burgundy in my cellar of 2800

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I was a regular buyer but largely stopped buying any 1994 or later Cote D’Or whites after the 2004 vintage except an occasional bottle for young drinking and an occasional good deal on a Premier Cru (knowing it’s a crap shoot). In retrospect, the first premox wine I recall buying and drinking, because no one knew it was premox at the time, was 1996 Sauzet Batard. (My premoxed Ramonets were in the cellar for the “long haul” at the time.) I remember being so excited to buy and later try, and so disappointed to drink, my first bottle of Sauzet Batard (and sell the second).

I still have random bottles of 1996, 97, 99, and 02 PCs and GCs in the cellar, but those are running out. And I have a few 2004s (older son’s birth year) with no expectations — Coche village Meursault, Niellon and J-M Pillot Chassagne Premier Crus, Carillon P-M Perrieres, Bouchard CC.

I caved by buying some 2014 Rollin Pernands a couple months ago to cellar. I think a lot of us Burg lovers were tempted by the 2014 Côte de Beaune whites. I love them (and the recently tasted 2011s) but I won’t waste my money on cellaring them.

I still buy Chablis to cellar (though not Dauvissat after becoming disenchanted by premox in wines that were spectacular on release). I also buy Maconnais whites - Thevenet’s wines and occasionally Barraud.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: Yes. I don’t buy any that need aging. Therefore, I buy very little.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Shannon Perdue
posts.cooked: Yes - the white burgundies I buy are consumed younger as it’s not worth the valuable cellar space for premoxed wines. It’s sad but reality these days…

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:

Yes. I don’t buy any that need aging. Therefore, I buy very little.

Yeah, pretty much. Still buy a few bottles, but not many.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Chris Seiber:

As a general statement, they don’t need age nearly as much these days anyway. Most white Burgs are ripe, open, and not scorchingly acidic nowadays - there just isn’t a strong incentive to age them into No Man’s Land anymore.

I am really closest to your comment here, Counsellor. And no more telling example than my last white burg. That was a 2012 Jadot Duc de Magenta Chassagne Morgeots Clos de la Chappelle. I decanted it for 1 hour. I exclaimed on tasting “this is why one drinks white wine”. And 3 days later, out of a recorked half, it had fanned out gorgeously and become everything I’d ever want in archetypal Chassagne.

And in this? Maybe, just maybe, a tacit admission and change by makers of these wines to make them at least closer to their top enjoyment level at a much earlier age than in the past.

Regardless, I am fairly committed now to treating pretty much all my higher-level white burg in this manner----decant, try some on night of opening, recork and drink majority 2 - 3 days later. I’ll experiment with this “technique” some more, but boy did it reap Joker’s wild dividends with this wine. A counter-example? A 2008 Bouchard Meursault Genevrieres, which was a rockstar for the first 5 or 6 bottles I had? The last one drunk about 3 weeks ago…already starting to tip over. Had lost much of the magic. YMMV, but that’s where I am right now. At this point, I can’t see needing to age any current-bought white burg more than 10 years, except maybe a Raveneau Grand Cru or Coche CC from 12 or before.

Slainte,

Mike

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Mike Grammer:

I decanted it for 1 hour. I exclaimed on tasting “this is why one drinks white wine”. Regardless, I am fairly committed now to treating pretty much all my higher-level white burg in this manner----decant, try some on night of opening, recork and drink majority 2 - 3 days later. I’ll experiment with this “technique” some more, but boy did it reap Joker’s wild dividends with this wine. A counter-example? A 2008 Bouchard Meursault Genevrieres, which was a rockstar for the first 5 or 6 bottles I had? The last one drunk about 3 weeks ago…already starting to tip over. Had lost much of the magic. YMMV, but that’s where I am right now. At this point, I can’t see needing to age any current-bought white burg more than 10 years, except maybe a Raveneau Grand Cru or Coche CC from 12 or before.

Slainte,

Mike

Yep, agree 100%.

I wonder, too, if most of these wines of today which are so much friendlier at a young age would actually develop as well as pre 1996 bottles anyway, even for the bottles that avoid premox. My recollection is that those pre 1996 wines were much different in their youth than their counterparts today – more taut, more sharp, much more acidic, less ripe fruit. Though I was just getting into wine around then, so my impressions could be colored by my newness.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: The problem with making wines that are more accessible in their youth is that they simply don’t develop those deep, complex, and unique flavors and aromas that come about only as a result of a long aging process. Are such wines “more drinkable at an earlier age”? Sure. But that’s not really the point.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: For GC’s I’m looking to drink the first bottle after 6-7 years, village and 1ers a bit earlier then going from there. Love white Burgs enough to put up with variability. Dry Germans are not a replacement.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. I also buy Ramonet, hoping they’ve fixed the problems and believing that the highs are high enough to justify the risk at the very reasonable UK allocation pricing. Dauvissat too, which I don’t mind drinking on the younger side. And I’m buying a lot of Guffens-Heynon Mâconnais wines, which have the cut and amplitude of the Côte de Beaune’s best but also show a lot of what they have to give after just five or six years (though my experience with vintages from the 1990s has been positive too). The best producers in the Mâconnais and Côte Chalonnaise have a lot to offer in the premox era, as the problem has neutralized many of the Côte de Beaune’s hypothetical advantages.

Very interesting that Guffens-Heynon ages, but Verget was the poster child for premox

posts.name: Lawrence Leichtman
posts.cooked: I don’t hold any more than 5-7 years and don’t buy any Grand Crus.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
This is a painful question and I want to give a detailed answer. It’s long, please bear with me.

The quick answer is that in the past 20 years my purchases of white Burgundy have declined by 2/3. The age at which I drink white Burgundy has declined by almost half. The decrease in age has to do with the inherent ageworthiness of the wines I am drinking (as opposed to those I drank before the premox plague and the huge price increases) as much as premox itself; ie, I am buying lesser Appellations. In the past 40 years my income has probably increased by an order of magnitude. I would say that the prices of top Burgundy has gone up by that much or more… can you imagine what the price of top Puligny would be if most of it didn’t suffer from premox?!?

I import some white Burgundy and will not name those producers. They are mostly in minor Appellations. This is one time that my policy of not writing about wines I sell is irritating, as some of them make moderately ageworthy wines that rarely if ever suffer from premox. But that is a slippery slope I won’t go down.

My life has been entwined with wine. Since 1980 it has provided a living for me and my family, as well as providing me a great deal of pleasure. I have been so lucky that for the first 35 years of my wine career, per capita consumption in the U.S. rose steadily by well over 2% a year (it has since seen tiny declines, but not among the types of wines I sell, which are still enjoying market growth). Also, in my opinion there has been a strong, steady, almost universal increase in wine quality in all categories. The one glaring exception has been white Burgundy, where quality has declined to the point where I can say that the category as I knew and enjoyed it from the ‘70s to the ‘90s simply no longer exists… and there is nothing that can take its place.

I no longer follow the Premox wiki, excellent as it is. With rare exceptions, GC white Burg and Premier Crus from Meursault, Puligny and Chassagne are simply no longer of interest. When I started drinking white Burg 40+ years ago I would regularly drink Village, lieux-dit and 1er Crus from the three mentioned Villages at 10 – 20 years of age. Paying for it was a strain on my budget I was willing to endure. As my taste in reds migrated from Bordeaux to Burgundy and began to encompass Rhone, Piedmont and even (actually always) California Cabernets (and a whole lot of other reds), Burgundy remained my benchmark for white wine.

There is no substitute for the quality of wines that used to be produced in Puligny, Chassagne and Meursault. However there are still plenty of fine white wine produced in Burgundy and a lot of it will mature nicely to ten years, even if there is so very little that can make it to twenty.

From north to south:

Chablis has been affected by premox, but that’s only one part of a trifecta also involving increased demand and some miserably miserly harvests. I represent a small grower whose wines still easily pass the 10 year threshhold. The oldest bottles in my basement from this Domaine are a few ’94 Clos, but I still have a nice smattering of ‘96s going strong. I wish I had bought more through the ’00 decade, but I didn’t. I’m OK from ’09 forward. It’s such a big district and so varied and variable that I won’t write more, except that I do enjoy Raveneau in French restaurants; more available and cheaper than at US retail.

There is very little white in the Cote de Nuits and it tends to be very expensive.

My favorite hunting ground for white Burgundy today is the Cote de Beaune north of Meursault. There are not a lot of whites from Beaune, Savigny and Pernand, but they are not rare and can be excellent (often more mineral than those from the ‘big 3’); they are reasonably priced and in my experience can very often improve up to ten years and sometimes even more. Thanks to some trade connections, I can occasionally get whites from Chandon des Briailles, great wines but expensive even with a good deal.

The Cote Chalonnaise is a go-to. Two nights ago I had an ’07 Rully 1er Cru from a grower I represent. It still was pretty fresh, although I will drink my remaining bottles over the next year. Curiously, the hot vintage ‘09s seem much younger than the two year difference would indicate. My last bottle of ’05 will be a special occasion next year. Mercurey and Givry are not as interesting IMO and have a lot less white. Montagny is all white, but I rarely find them all that good (please let me know if I’m missing something!).

Pouilly Fuisse and its satellites can produce anything from barely Macon quality to almost Meursault. Chateau de Fuisse is as good as it should be at all levels, but not cheap at the upper ones. Saint-Veran can be good; IMO Vire-Clesse even better. Within the oceans of Macon there is a lot of good wine (and a lot of mediocre wine), but little that I would want past 5 years, much less 10 (again, please educate me if I’m missing something).

There is a lot of good white Bourgogne from producers all through Burgundy, but not necessarily either consistent in quality or easy to find.

Past Burgundy, I drink a fair amount of California Chardonnay. Despite some very expensive, highly rated and prestigious bottlings from North Coast Appellations, I find Mount Eden hands down California’s best producer. Ridge also does amazingly well. The other 2 of my ‘3M’s are Chateau Montelena and Mayacamas. Like Mount Eden and Ridge, both very easily pass the test of improving the bottle for 10+ years. The glass of 1999 Mayacamas I’m drinking right now with smoked salmon from Smith’s Log Smoke House is ample evidence.

At 71 years of age, even at these prices, I would still buy Meursault, Chassagne and Puligny to lay down for 10 years (somebody else would drink them if I could not), but not without confidence that the wines will improve for 10+ years. So except for the aforementioned whites from Chandon des Briailles, I’m not buying top white Burgundy. This makes me sad.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Dan, thanks for taking the time to lay out your thoughts. It’s a real shame what has happened, but I am still hopeful that it is reversible although I will not bet my hard-earned income that the necessary change has already happened.

In Macon, as I mentioned above, Jean Thevenet (and his family) make excellent wine in Vire-Clesse. Both Bongran and Roally (including from predecessor Henri Goyard) are ageworthy. 1996s, 1997s, and 2002s are drinking wonderfully within my experience, having developed positively beyond their young selves. 1995 was a botrytis year, and I don’t know how it has developed but it had a lot of potential young. I have not seen any evidence of premox. It is not the dirt of Meursault Perrieres or Puligny Caillerets. But it’s very good wine.

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked: Thanks, Dan, for your post.

posts.name: Peter Papay
posts.cooked:
Hi Joel,

Great to see you contributing to the forum.

I have mostly been abstaining from buying aged white Burgundy for Premix reasons and just not willing to play the “Russian Roulette” game at those prices. I did start buying 2014 vintages with Diam closures and future will show how that will work out. In the meanwhile I get my thirst for aged chard quenched by local producers (Lenko and …)

Thank you Dan for your thoughtful insight.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
to Jason Cohen,

Thanks for the suggestions. I know and admire both Thevenet and Bongran. However I have never had Roally. I will look for the wines next time I leave Maine.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
Jayson Cohen:

Dan, thanks for taking the time to lay out your thoughts. It’s a real shame what has happened, but I am still hopeful that it is reversible although I will not bet my hard-earned income that the necessary change has already happened.

In Macon, as I mentioned above, Jean Thevenet (and his family) make excellent wine in Vire-Clesse. Both Bongran and Roally (including from predecessor Henri Goyard) are ageworthy. 1996s, 1997s, and 2002s are drinking wonderfully within my experience, having developed positively beyond their young selves. 1995 was a botrytis year, and I don’t know how it has developed but it had a lot of potential young. I have not seen any evidence of premox. It is not the dirt of Meursault Perrieres or Puligny Caillerets. But it’s very good wine.

I don’t know how I missed this, but I agree and have had the same experience. I’ve also had a delicious recent 1996 Guillemot-Michel, another Macon traditionalist producer with no fear of a little botrytis.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
William Kelley:

This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. I also buy Ramonet, hoping they’ve fixed the problems and believing that the highs are high enough to justify the risk at the very reasonable UK allocation pricing. Dauvissat too, which I don’t mind drinking on the younger side. And I’m buying a lot of Guffens-Heynen Mâconnais wines, which have the cut and amplitude of the Côte de Beaune’s best but also show a lot of what they have to give after just five or six years (though my experience with vintages from the 1990s has been positive too). The best producers in the Mâconnais and Côte Chalonnaise have a lot to offer in the premox era, as the problem has neutralized many of the Côte de Beaune’s hypothetical advantages.

Very interesting that Guffens-Heynon ages, but Verget was the poster child for premox

This is an interesting question. I’ve actually had very good luck with old Verget, including 1992, 1995 and 1996 Enseignères, 1995 Valmur and 1995 Purozots, all aging gracefully this year. But I accept that they had a lot of problems, and that many people were badly burned. I’ve spoken to Jean-Marie about it, and his contention is that the problem is caused above all by fatiguing the wine with excessive élevage in wood, especially new wood. Of course, how much is too much depends on the wine in question.

As for Guffens-Heynen, their 1997s, 1999s and 2000s are all drinking very well right now.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Dan Kravitz:

to Jason Cohen,

Thanks for the suggestions. I know and admire both Thevenet and Bongran. However I have never had Roally. I will look for the wines next time I leave Maine.

Dan Kravitz

The Roally wines are indeed excellent, and released a bit earlier than the Bongran and Gillet bottlings. The 2014 is current, whereas Bongran is just moving onto 2012.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Have cut back considerably. Frankly, more due to escalating prices than to premox worries, but couple the two and it becomes very difficult to pull the trigger on most of the traditionally bigger names.

Fortunately, there are so many other white wines that give great drinking pleasure, it’s not really a problem to find affordable replacements. But you have to be willing to look beyond Chardonnay, and the elitism of Burgundy.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
to Alan Rath,

I very often look way beyond Chardonnay, although I may be guilty of Burgundy elitism.

What are your suggestions?

Thanks.


Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Hi Dan,

Just a little jab at those few people for whom Burgundy is the only wine they will drink Of course I love Burgundy, but more and more it’s too expensive to buy and drink regularly. We drink Chenin Blanc, Sauvignon Blanc, Albariño, white Rhones, Riesling, Gewurz, Arneis, Carricante, just to name a few, and that leaves probably a couple hundred other white wines to explore - as I know you know better than I

On our recent visits to Venice and Piemonte, we drank several Arneis and Soave wines that were delicious and relatively affordable even here. Now, those aren’t going to age and deliver quite the experience of a great white Burg, but if I could never drink another Burg I would find plenty of excellent white wines to satisfy my thirst.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: The only aged wines that have rung my bells like white Burgundy have been a few Savennieres, but overall I think it’s more of a crapshoot than aging Burgundy. Savennieres sometimes seems to have about a three month window somewhere between age 10 and 25 where it is stellar, and the rest of the time just okay (and some have oxidation problems too). At least when top notch Burgundies aren’t oxidized, they are fairly reliably good.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Aged Rieslings can be outstanding.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Aged Riesling. Aged Vouvray. Aged Saumur Blanc. Aged Chavignol. Aged white Bordeaux. Aged white Rioja. Aged Gruner (not my favorite).

But premox is not just a Burg issue.

And none of these is a replacement for aged Cote de Beaune whites. The wine world has lost something tangible.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
A 1999 Pieropan Soave La Rocca recently was brilliant. Stunning, with some nuttiness and a little butterscotch showing its age, but loaded with flowers and melon with a touch of slate, and still with enough acidity to hold it together. Age improved it.

I’ve been loving my Austrian Rieslings and GVs from the late 1990s, and quite a few 1990s Alsatians from ZH, Schlumberger, Kreydenweiss, and Weinbach.

There have been some premox issues, but Loire Chenins from a number of producers and styles are aging well, as have Sauvignon Blancs from Thomas Labaille and Clos Roche Blanche.

Dan, I’ll plug your Chablis producer for you. In 2015 I my last bottle of the 1995 Caves Duplessis Chablis 1er Cru Montée de Tonnerre was drinking well, while the Les Clos was terrific and I still have 2 left.

William Kelley:
Howard Cooper:
William Kelley:

This is a difficult one. My response is to buy pre-1995 white Burgundy, and a handful of lower-risk producers: Bernard Moreau, PYCM, Raveneau, Coche-Dury (though I am very disappointed by Raphael’s wines from 2013 onwards), Domaine de Bongran. I also buy Ramonet, hoping they’ve fixed the problems and believing that the highs are high enough to justify the risk at the very reasonable UK allocation pricing. Dauvissat too, which I don’t mind drinking on the younger side. And I’m buying a lot of Guffens-Heynen Mâconnais wines, which have the cut and amplitude of the Côte de Beaune’s best but also show a lot of what they have to give after just five or six years (though my experience with vintages from the 1990s has been positive too). The best producers in the Mâconnais and Côte Chalonnaise have a lot to offer in the premox era, as the problem has neutralized many of the Côte de Beaune’s hypothetical advantages.

Very interesting that Guffens-Heynon ages, but Verget was the poster child for premox

This is an interesting question. I’ve actually had very good luck with old Verget, including 1992, 1995 and 1996 Enseignères, 1995 Valmur and 1995 Purozots, all aging gracefully this year. But I accept that they had a lot of problems, and that many people were badly burned. I’ve spoken to Jean-Marie about it, and his contention is that the problem is caused above all by fatiguing the wine with excessive élevage in wood, especially new wood. Of course, how much is too much depends on the wine in question.

As for Guffens-Heynen, their 1997s, 1999s and 2000s are all drinking very well right now.

The Verget PremOx question was a complicated one for me. I bought a lot of Chablis and a little Cote de Beaune white from 95-97, and ended up ripping through most of them at about 10 years of age, and never had a single bad bottle. It actually made me an early PremOx skeptic. And then I found other wines and I wasn’t. No doubt people found trouble with Verget, but I was very lucky.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: A friend has picked up a bunch of 1996 and 1997 Vergets (mostly Grand Cru Chablis but at least one Batard) at auction and almost all have escaped the premox plague. I wish his venture into 2007 and 2008 Fevre Grand Crus turned out as well.

posts.name: Chris. Mc Cart
posts.cooked: Mike, glad you mentioned it. Love Pieropan’s La Rocca Soave with a few years on them. Still reasonable to find & buy.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
As it sat more in memory, a truly unforgettable wine. I still think of it from time to time. From 6 years ago in D.C.

"1992 Verget Chevalier Montrachet

It is not oxidized. Hurrah! So different from the others—exotic flowers and some tropical fruits in the bouquet. Le gout continues this theme, with a delicious toffee undertone and carrying spiciness, together with a swirl of lactic/sour cream (or yogurt, maybe) and yellow fruit. Reminds Dean of a Mount Eden which I can see. Even better, it holds form over 2 hours in the glass. What comes through here is a sense beyond mere tasting notes—a sense of class and tremendous interest, #2 tonight. "

And from Atlanta a couple years ago:

"2004 Verget Corton Charlemagne

We had to pull this after another Girardin (99) was sadly poxed. This is different again from the first three, blending pear, nips of vanilla, stone and perhaps light peach into the aromatics. Le gout shows plenty of verve and strictness, but front and back-end citrus and apple are quite identifiable. Needs a few more years."

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Perhaps Jean-Marie Guffens’ biggest sin was not suffering from premox, but irritating all his colleagues. Premox made it easy to dismiss his body of work out-of-hand…

His 1995 Verget Valmur “Sud” (rumored to be part Les Clos, part Valmur) was the best bottle of Chablis I tasted this year.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
I’ve always been a big Macon fan, which, because of the stainless steel fermentations, haven’t been affectetd by Premox (and I drink them young, I mean, real young).

When I do buy a white from Beaune, I usually drink them within a year -

fancy_title: Has Premox changed your White Burgundy buying strategy?
title: Has Premox changed your White Burgundy buying strategy?

participants.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
